name: Abhijat Sinha
useCase: I am on a mission to enable Autonomous Web3. A key step toward this is combining AI-driven reasoning with deterministic execution - where agents handle planning, strategy, and adaptation, while Decimal (https://decimal.at) guarantees reliable execution through programmable workflows. We would deploy the AI agent as a workflow intelligence layer within Decimal, allowing users to express intent in natural language (e.g., “rebalance my portfolio across chains when yield drops”). The agent would design, optimize, and deploy executable workflows, continuously monitoring on-chain conditions and improving strategies over time. Decimal would act as the execution engine, ensuring all actions follow predefined rules and guardrails, enabling safe, verifiable, and autonomous operation. Over time, this foundation can evolve into self-sustaining agents that not only manage workflows independently but also generate consistent, programmable value for their users.
whySelected: I’ve spent 20+ years building large-scale systems across global enterprises, and over the past few years in Web3, I’ve seen firsthand that the biggest bottleneck is not infrastructure but coordination. As AI agents and on-chain strategies grow more complex, reliable coordinated execution becomes the bottleneck. Decimal is a direct response to that problem. Over the last year, I rebuilt the platform from the ground up to focus on workflow-driven automation, and we already have 800+ early users actively exploring real use cases across chains and protocols. This isn’t a theoretical idea, it’s being shaped by actual builders and operators. What makes this particularly important now is the convergence of AI agents and programmable finance. Agents can generate strategies, but without a reliable execution layer, they remain incomplete. Decimal is designed to be that missing layer. This matters to me because I believe Web3 will only scale when systems can operate autonomously, not through manual coordination, but through programmable, reliable execution. That’s what we’re building toward. From reactive, emotional decisions to structured, predictable execution.

name: Ankit Jain
role: Senior Data Scientist at Eli Lilly and Company
useCase: I would build PharmaSafe Agent — an AI agent that automates pharmacovigilance (drug safety) case processing for small and mid-size pharmaceutical companies. Every drug on the market generates safety reports called ICSRs (Individual Case Safety Reports) when patients experience adverse reactions. Regulators like the FDA mandate these be processed and submitted within 7-15 days. The problem? Each case requires a trained specialist to manually read unstructured patient narratives, extract structured data, code adverse events using medical terminology (MedDRA), assess causality, write a regulatory narrative, and submit in compliant format. This takes 30-45 minutes per case. Large pharma companies have massive teams for this. Small and mid-size companies don't — they outsource to CROs at $80-200 per case, with costs that spike unpredictably during product launches. PharmaSafe Agent would handle this end-to-end: Monitor incoming reports from email, call centers, and literature feeds Validate each report against the four minimum ICSR criteria and triage by seriousness Extract patient demographics, medical history, suspect drug details, and adverse event data from unstructured text using fine-tuned LLMs Code adverse events to MedDRA terms and perform preliminary causality assessment Draft a regulatory-quality case narrative following ICH E2B(R3) conventions Track every case against its regulatory deadline and escalate overdue cases Package approved cases into submission-ready E2B(R3) XML for FDA/EMA The agent follows an "AI prepares, human approves" model — it never submits without a pharmacovigilance specialist's sign-off. This shifts the specialist's role from data entry to clinical judgment, making one person as productive as five. Target impact: processing time from 30-45 min to 5-8 min per case, cost reduction of 70-80%, and 99%+ deadline compliance.
whySelected: I'm not pitching a hypothetical — I'm already building this at Eli Lilly and Company. As an AI/ML engineer at Lilly, my primary project (AEPC) classifies pharmaceutical chatbot conversations for Mounjaro and Zepbound for regulatory safety reporting. I built the full production pipeline: data standardization, LLM fine-tuning based adverse event classification, and a validated questionnaire-based approach where AI populates clinically validated forms rather than making direct safety determinations. This design distributes AI error risk across multiple validated checkpoints — a strategy born from navigating real FDA regulatory constraints, not academic theory. I've documented the entire architecture across 13,700+ lines of technical documentation covering pipeline design, validation checks, edge cases, and deployment paths for fine-tuned LLMs on Azure infrastructure. What makes me different from other applicants: Domain depth: I understand MedDRA coding, ICH E2B(R3) submissions, causality assessment, and ICSR timelines — not just the ML techniques. This is what makes the agent actually deployable vs. a hackathon prototype. Production experience: I've shipped pharmacovigilance AI at one of the world's top 10 pharma companies. I know what breaks in production, what regulators scrutinize, and what auditors look for. Strategic design thinking: My "AI populates validated forms" approach was engineered to satisfy regulatory accountability requirements. In pharma, how you frame AI's role matters as much as what it does. Personal motivation: Every ICSR processed on time is a safety signal reaching regulators faster. I've seen the backlog from inside big pharma — I want to solve it for the companies that need it most but can afford it least. This scholarship would let me extend battle-tested work to the broader pharma ecosystem.

name: Chewe Kambobe
useCase: I would deploy an AI-powered Product Safety Alert Agent that solves the last-mile gap between official recall information and everyday consumers. While product recalls for food, medicines, vehicles, and electronics are regularly published by regulators and manufacturers, this information is fragmented and rarely reaches the people most at risk. The agent would continuously monitor verified sources (regulatory bodies, manufacturers, safety databases), extract and structure recall data, and deliver it in real time through low-friction channels. A key feature would be a browser-based assistant that alerts users while they browse or interact with products online. For example, if a user searches for or views a recalled product, the agent would flag it instantly and provide clear guidance, without requiring sign-ups or personal data. In addition, users could query the agent directly (“Is this medicine safe?”), and receive verified, simplified answers. The goal is not to replace existing systems, but to make them usable transforming scattered safety notices into accessible, real-time protection.
whySelected: This idea targets a clear failure in how critical safety information reaches people. Product recalls already exist, but they are passive, fragmented, and easy to miss. The risk is not theoretical, people continue using unsafe products simply because they are unaware. What I bring is a focus on execution and usability. The approach is not to build another information source, but to deliver verified safety alerts directly within everyday digital behavior, especially through a browser-based interface that requires no sign-ups or technical knowledge. The solution is practical and deployable with existing tools. My priority would be to build a working agent that integrates reliable data sources, filters for relevance, and delivers clear, actionable alerts. Success would be measured by whether users actually receive and act on safety information. This has immediate public value and strong potential to scale across regions and sectors.
